Bringing in the ark marked the end that needed to complete the work; this was done with great solemnity. The ark was placed in the designated spot inside the temple, where they expected God to speak to them, even in the most holy place. The poles of the ark were extended so as to direct the high priest to the mercy seat above the ark when he entered once a year to sprinkle blood there; thus they remained useful, though no longer needed for carrying it. The appearance of God’s glory in a cloud may signify, 1. The darkness of that period, compared with the light of the gospel, by which we behold, as in a mirror, the glory of the Lord. 2. The darkness of our present state, compared with the sight of God, which will be the happiness of heaven, where the Divine glory is revealed.

Solomon encouraged the priests, who were astonished at the dark cloud. Dark providences should prompt us to flee for refuge to the hope of the gospel. Nothing can more reconcile us to them than to consider what God has said and to compare his word and works together. Whatever good we do, we must view it as the fulfillment of God's promise to us, not of our promises to him.

In this excellent prayer, Solomon does as we should in every prayer; he gives glory to God. New experiences of the truth of God’s promises call for greater praise. He asks for grace and favor from God. The experiences we have of God fulfilling his promises should encourage us to depend on them and plead them with him; and those who expect further mercies must be thankful for former ones. God's promises must guide our desires and be the basis of our hopes and expectations in prayer. The sacrifices, incense, and entire service of the temple were all symbolic of the Redeemer’s offices, offering, and intercession. Therefore, the temple should always be remembered. Under one word, “forgive,” Solomon expressed everything he could ask on behalf of his people. For, as all misery comes from sin, forgiveness of sin prepares the way for the removal of every evil and the receiving of every good. Without it, no deliverance can prove a blessing. In addition to the teaching of the word of God, Solomon asked the Lord himself to teach the people to benefit from everything, even their punishments. They will know each man the plague of his own heart, what pains him; and they will spread their hands in prayer toward this house; whether the trouble is of body or mind, they will represent it before God. Inward burdens seem especially meant. Sin is the plague of our own hearts; our inherent corruptions are our spiritual diseases: every true Israelite strives to know these so that he may mortify them and watch against their rise. These drive him to his knees; lamenting them, he spreads forth his hands in prayer. After many details, Solomon concludes with the general request that God would listen to his praying people. Now, under the gospel, no place can add to the prayers made in or toward it. The essence is Christ; whatever we ask in his name, it will be given us. In this way the Israel of God is established and sanctified, the backslider is recovered and healed. In this way the stranger is brought near, the mourner is comforted, the name of God is glorified. Sin is the cause of all our troubles; repentance and forgiveness lead to all human happiness.

Never was a congregation dismissed with something more likely to affect them and remain with them. What Solomon asks for in this prayer is still granted in Christ’s intercession, of which his supplication was a type. We will receive grace sufficient, suitable, and seasonable in every time of need. No human heart is naturally willing to obey the gospel call to repentance, faith, and newness of life, walking in all the commandments of the Lord, yet Solomon encourages the people to be perfect. This is the scriptural method; it is our duty to obey the command of the law and the call of the gospel, seeing we have broken the law. When our hearts are inclined thereto, feeling our sinfulness and weakness, we pray for Divine assistance; thus we are made able to serve God through Jesus Christ.
Solomon offered a great sacrifice. He kept the feast of tabernacles, as it appears, after the feast of dedication. Thus we should go home rejoicing from holy ordinances, thankful for God’s goodness.
